paper_title: Enhanced photoresponse under visible light in Pt ionized TiO2 nanotube for the photocatalytic splitting of water

catalyst.source: Enhanced photoresponse under visible light in Pt ionized TiO2 nanotube for the photocatalytic splitting of water
catalyst.context: Enhanced photoresponse under visible light in Pt ionized TiO2 nanotube for the photocatalytic splitting of water

catalyst.llm generation: Platinum ion-exchanged titania nanotubes (Pt(IE)TiNT) / Platinum nanoparticles on titania nanotubes
catalyst.source: We report a new material, platinum ion-exchanged titania nanotubes (Pt(IE)TiNT) synthesized by hydrothermal method, was found to be active under visible light irradiation.The platinum nanoparticles well embeded in nanotube matrixes in the ionized samples were highly photoactive materials for the stoichiometric production of hydrogen and oxygen by the splitting of water under visible light at one sun, resulting in hydrogen evolution rates of 14.6 and 2.3 lmolh in aqueous methanol and pure water, respectively.
catalyst.context: We report a new material, platinum ion-exchanged titania nanotubes (Pt(IE)TiNT) synthesized by hydrothermal method, was found to be active under visible light irradiation.The platinum nanoparticles well embeded in nanotube matrixes in the ionized samples were highly photoactive materials for the stoichiometric production of hydrogen and oxygen by the splitting of water under visible light at one sun, resulting in hydrogen evolution rates of 14.6 and 2.3 lmolh in aqueous methanol and pure water, respectively.

catalyst.source: The titania nanotube (TiNT) was prepared using a similar method to that described by Kasuga et al. 15, except the hydrothermal temperature in an aqueous NaOH (8 M, 100 ml) was maintained at 130 C for 20 h 14.
catalyst.context: The titania nanotube (TiNT) was prepared using a similar method to that described by Kasuga et al. 15, except the hydrothermal temperature in an aqueous NaOH (8 M, 100 ml) was maintained at 130 C for 20 h 14.The slurry was filtrated, washed, dried (80 C) and calcined (250 C) in air.Pt was introduced onto the TiNT using an ion exchange method, employing an aqueous ammonia solution of platinum tetraamine Pt(II) chloride hydrate (Pt(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 A H 2 O), at pH 10, with continuous stirring for 20 h.The Pt ion-exchanged nanotube powder was carefully washed with DI water and calcined at 250 C, which was denoted as Pt(IE)TiNT.For comparison, Pt metal was also introduced onto the nanotube using the incipient wetness method with an aqueous solution of hydrogen hexachloroplatinate(IV) acid (H 2 PtCl 6 A 6 H 2 O), with the same loading amount and then calcined at 250 C, which was denoted as Pt(Imp)TiNT. The photocatalytic water splitting reaction was carried out with 0.5 g of fine powder photocatalysts in an outer irradiation Pyrex cell (300 ml) reaction system under the visible light (k 400 nm) irradiation using an ozone free Xe arc lamp (300 W, Hamamatsu: L 2479) attached with UV cut filter (FSQ-GG400 minimum spectral range of transmittance k 1 (4 0 0) bought from Newport Corporation) at one sun (AM 1.5).The details of the photocatalytic water splitting procedures have been described in our previous work except the substitution of UV light source to visible light and the type of reactor used 12. The prepared samples were characterized by high resolution transmission electron micorscopy (HR-TEM), Philips TECNAI-F20, at accelerated voltage 160 kV), X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD, Rigaku X-ray diffractometer, operating at 40 kV and 40 mA using Cu Ka radiations), UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-vis DRS, Shimadzu-UV525).Photocurrent density was measured by using a scanning potentiostate (EG G273), with the software (EG GM542).BET surface area (Micromeritics ASAP 2100) was obtained with N 2 adsorption at temperature of 77.40 K.

catalyst.source: The photocurrent densities were measured using the catalysts film as the working electrodes, the Pt wire as the counter electrode and an AgAgCl3 M NaCl reference electrode, in an electrochemical cell (10 ml), with pH 9, 0.1 M Na 2 SO 3 as the electrolyte, under visible light.
catalyst.context: Fig. 1 shows the high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Philips Technai 160 kV) images, with inset images of the field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Hitachi 4700).HR-TEM images clearly indicated that the original nanotubes morphologies were maintained, with 8 nm outside diameter and 5 nm inner diameter for the TiNT.In Pt(IE)TiNT, with platinum particles of about 2 nm size, well embedded in the texture of the titania tubular structure.However, around 12 nm large Pt clusters were formed on the Pt(Imp)TiNT, as shown in Fig. 1.TiNT substrate consisted of titanium, oxygen and sodium with the atomic ratio of 35:62.3:2.7, with a titania to oxygen atomic ratio of 1.78, are shown in Table 1.After doping about 1.8 wt Pt on TiNT employing ion-exchange, negligible amounts of sodium ions existed, indicating that almost all of the sodium ions were ion-exchanged by the Pt ions in the ion-exchange procedure.However, more than 70 of the sodium metal remained in the TiNT even after impregnating 1.8 wt Pt metal onto the Pt(Imp)TiNT.Table 1 shows the band gap energies of the samples, as measured by UV-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-vis DRS, Shimadzu-UV525) analysis.The Pt(IE) TiNT showed a reduced band gap energy due to the significant absorption in visible light region up to 500 nm, in contrast to the absorption edges at around 400 nm on the TiO 2 (P-25), TiNT and Pt(Imp)TiNT as shown in Fig. 2. A red shifted photoresponse of Pt(IE)TiNT may lead to high photocatalytic activity under visible light.The band gap was further reconfirmed by Schuster-Kubelka-Munk (SKM) remission functional relations as where (R 1 ) is diffuse reflectance, (K) is absorption and (S) is scattering coefficients.At constant S, F SKM (R 1 ) l K and a plot of F SKM (R 1 ) vs. concentration of absorbing species was straight line passing through the origin.This linear relationship has been used to determine the band gap in the Pt(IE)TiNT and was found to be 2.48 eV 16. From the X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku with Cu Ka radiation) patterns of TiNT and ionized Pt(IE)TiNT as shown in Fig. 3, no apparent structural differences were observed either before or after the photocatalytic reaction run (for 36 h) over platinum ionized Pt(IE)TiNT, indicating its high stability in the photocatalytic water splitting reaction.Strong and sharp anatase titania peaks were observed at the 2-theta's of 25.3, 38.0, 48.3 and 54.8, 55.8, 62.58, 67.21, 68.79, 75.18 corresponding to (1 0 1), (0 0 4), (2 0 0), (1 0 5), (2 1 1), (2 0 4), (1 1 6), (2 2 0), (2 1 5) phases, respectively and a rutile peak at the 2-theta of 28.8.As shown in Table 1, the BET (Micromeritics ASAP 2100) surface areas decreased slightly and significantly on the Pt(IE)TiNT and Pt(Imp)TiNT, respectively.Fig. 4 shows the photocurrent densities of the catalysts, as measured using a scanning potentiostate (EG G273), with the software (EG GM542), as reported elsewhere 17.The photocurrent densities were measured using the catalysts film as the working electrodes, the Pt wire as the counter electrode and an AgAgCl3 M NaCl reference electrode, in an electrochemical cell (10 ml), with pH 9, 0.1 M Na 2 SO 3 as the electrolyte, under visible light.As describe in the literature 17, photocurrent densities are proportional to the number of photo formed electrons.Bare TiNT shows very low photocurrent densities under one sun (100 mWcm 2 ).The photocurrent densities of Pt(IE)TiNT are much higher than those of Pt(Imp)TiNT, even though they contain similar amounts of platinum.The possibility of sulphate ion acts as scavenger and might hamper in the photocurrent generation can be deducted since the applied potential for the all the samples are the same (0.5 V), however in dark potential all samples response to almost zero.These results suggest that presence of platinum particles and their sizes in Pt(IE)TiNT matrix has much influence in photocurrent generation attributed to the nanosize of embedded platinum particles.As expected from the UV-DRS data, highly dispersed Pt clusters on Pt(IE)TiNT promoted a photoresponse under visible light, resulting in high photocurrent densities.However, a significant amount of photo excited electrons may be recombined on the large aggregated Pt clusters in Pt(imp)TiNT, which may act as the recombination centers 18.These photocurrent results were consistent with the photocatalytic activities required to produce hydrogen by the splitting of water under visible light, as shown in Fig. 5. Bare TiO 2 (P-25) and TiNT showed no activities for H 2 evolution in either pure water or aqueous methanol solution under visible light.Furthermore, just trace amounts of hydrogen were evolved with the Pt(Imp) TiNT and Pt(Imp)TiO 2 (P-25) in either aqueous methanol or pure water.However, it is noteworthy that the Pt ionized titania nanotube, Pt(IE)TiNT, was significantly active for the production of hydrogen under visible light, at rates of 14.6 and 2.3 lmol H 2 h in the aqueous methanol solution and pure water, respectively.The catalyst was not deactivated with over 36 h of running.The production of hydrogen stopped in the dark as the light was turned off, but resumed when the visible light was turned on again, indicating the hydrogen production is a photocatalytic process. The oxygen evolution rate with Pt(IE)TiNT was almost stoichiometric with the mole ratio of H 2 O 2 2. Bare TiO 2 (P-25) and TiNT showed no activities for O 2 evolution in either pure water or aqueous methanol solution under visible light (Fig. 6).Furthermore, just trace amount of O 2 was evolved with the Pt(Imp)TiNT and Pt(Imp)TiO 2 (P-25) in either aqueous methanol or pure water.However, it is noteworthy that the Pt ionized titania nanotube, Pt(IE)TiNT, release significant amount of O 2 under visible light, at rates of 7.3 and 1.1 lmol O 2 h in the aqueous methanol solution and pure water, respectively. Well dispersed Pt clusters into the TiNT texture, about 2 nm in size, reduced the band gap energy from 3.1 eV to 2.48 eV, which leads to a significant photoresponse under ca.500 nm visible light, resulting in a large number of photo excitons, as observed by the high photocurrent densities on the Pt(IE)TiNT.These exciton may be efficiently separated owing not only to the well dispersed Pt particles, which promote quick electron transfer, but also the reduced grain boundaries of the TiNT compared to nano particles.Oxygen deficiency (Table 1) in the TiNT is expected to generates a sub-band level over the valence band.The band gap transitions in Pt(IE)TiNT may be attributable to internal transitions in the Pt d shell.Influence of metal composite has shown to facilitate charge rectification in semiconductor nanostructures.The noble metal Pt can act as sink for photoinduced charge carriers, promotes interfacial charge transfer process and these metal nanoparticles are considered to be highly electronegative as their Fermi level can be shifted to negative potentials owing to charge accumulating effects.The introduced small Pt particles shift the Fermi level toward the conduction band, which leads to the reduced band gap energy.The Fermi level shift of a semiconductor particle to the conduction band is possible due to the accumulation of electrons.Therefore, this improves the overall energetics of the semiconductor system.A smaller sized Pt metal particle induces a greater shift in the E F than larger particles 19.However, large aggregated Pt particles in Pt(Imp) TiNT were located on the exterior surface of the TiNT, as shown in Fig. 1, resulting in no variation of the band structure and no photocatalytic activity for the splitting of water.The recombination of excitons may be drastically increased on the large aggregated Pt particles in Pt(Imp) TiNT, due to the significant Pt-Pt interaction 20 as well as the non-uniform distributions 20 of the Pt clusters, resulting in extremely low photo catalytic activities and photocurrent densities.Sodium ions may also act as recombination sites in Pt(Imp)TiNT, as reported by Fernandez et al. 21.

catalyst.source: In conclusion, the Pt ion-exchanged titania nanotube, Pt(IE)TiNT, was highly active for the stoichiometric production of hydrogen and oxygen, 14.6 (in methanol solution) and 2.3 (in pure water) lmol H 2 h from the splitting of water under visible light.
catalyst.context: In conclusion, the Pt ion-exchanged titania nanotube, Pt(IE)TiNT, was highly active for the stoichiometric production of hydrogen and oxygen, 14.6 (in methanol solution) and 2.3 (in pure water) lmol H 2 h from the splitting of water under visible light.Highly dispersed Pt clusters (about 2 nm size) were formed in the texture of the TiNT in Pt(IE)TiNT, which significantly reduced the band gap energy from 3.1 eV (bare TiNT) to 2.48 eV (Pt(IE)TiNT).However, the large aggregated Pt particles introduced into the Pt(Imp)TiNT, using the impregnated method, did not contribute to the reduced band gap energy, and resulted in no photocatalytic activity for the splitting of water under visible light. Fig. 1.HR-TEM and FE-SEM (inset) images of: (a) Pt(Imp) TiNT and (b) Pt(IE) TiNT. Fig. 2. UV-vis DRS spectra of: (a) P-25, (b) TiNT, (c) Pt(Imp)TiNT, and (d) Pt (IE) TiNT. Fig. 3. XRD patterns of: (a) TiNT as is, (b) Pt(Imp)TiNT, and (c) Pt(IE) TiNT used in the splitting of water for 36 h. Physico-chemical properties of TiNT and PtTiNTs Fig.6.O 2 evolution from the splitting of water over various photocatalysts under visible light with one sun. Fig.5.H 2 evolution from the splitting of water over various photocatalysts under visible light with one sun. We are greatfully acknowledge the support from Brain Korea 21 (BK21) program and Center for Ultramicrochemical Process System (CUPS).We thank to Mr. Jong-Gyun Kang for HR-TEM analysis in Jeon-ju branch of the Korea Basic Science Institute (KBSI).

co-catalyst.source: Pt was introduced onto the TiNT using an ion exchange method, employing an aqueous ammonia solution of platinum tetraamine Pt(II) chloride hydrate (Pt(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 A H 2 O), at pH 10, with continuous stirring for 20 h.
co-catalyst.context: The titania nanotube (TiNT) was prepared using a similar method to that described by Kasuga et al. 15, except the hydrothermal temperature in an aqueous NaOH (8 M, 100 ml) was maintained at 130 C for 20 h 14.The slurry was filtrated, washed, dried (80 C) and calcined (250 C) in air.Pt was introduced onto the TiNT using an ion exchange method, employing an aqueous ammonia solution of platinum tetraamine Pt(II) chloride hydrate (Pt(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 A H 2 O), at pH 10, with continuous stirring for 20 h.The Pt ion-exchanged nanotube powder was carefully washed with DI water and calcined at 250 C, which was denoted as Pt(IE)TiNT.For comparison, Pt metal was also introduced onto the nanotube using the incipient wetness method with an aqueous solution of hydrogen hexachloroplatinate(IV) acid (H 2 PtCl 6 A 6 H 2 O), with the same loading amount and then calcined at 250 C, which was denoted as Pt(Imp)TiNT. The photocatalytic water splitting reaction was carried out with 0.5 g of fine powder photocatalysts in an outer irradiation Pyrex cell (300 ml) reaction system under the visible light (k 400 nm) irradiation using an ozone free Xe arc lamp (300 W, Hamamatsu: L 2479) attached with UV cut filter (FSQ-GG400 minimum spectral range of transmittance k 1 (4 0 0) bought from Newport Corporation) at one sun (AM 1.5).The details of the photocatalytic water splitting procedures have been described in our previous work except the substitution of UV light source to visible light and the type of reactor used 12. The prepared samples were characterized by high resolution transmission electron micorscopy (HR-TEM), Philips TECNAI-F20, at accelerated voltage 160 kV), X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD, Rigaku X-ray diffractometer, operating at 40 kV and 40 mA using Cu Ka radiations), UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-vis DRS, Shimadzu-UV525).Photocurrent density was measured by using a scanning potentiostate (EG G273), with the software (EG GM542).BET surface area (Micromeritics ASAP 2100) was obtained with N 2 adsorption at temperature of 77.40 K.

co-catalyst.source: Highly dispersed Pt clusters (about 2 nm size) were formed in the texture of the TiNT in Pt(IE)TiNT, which significantly reduced the band gap energy from 3.1 eV (bare TiNT) to 2.48 eV (Pt(IE)TiNT).
co-catalyst.context: In conclusion, the Pt ion-exchanged titania nanotube, Pt(IE)TiNT, was highly active for the stoichiometric production of hydrogen and oxygen, 14.6 (in methanol solution) and 2.3 (in pure water) lmol H 2 h from the splitting of water under visible light.Highly dispersed Pt clusters (about 2 nm size) were formed in the texture of the TiNT in Pt(IE)TiNT, which significantly reduced the band gap energy from 3.1 eV (bare TiNT) to 2.48 eV (Pt(IE)TiNT).However, the large aggregated Pt particles introduced into the Pt(Imp)TiNT, using the impregnated method, did not contribute to the reduced band gap energy, and resulted in no photocatalytic activity for the splitting of water under visible light. Fig. 1.HR-TEM and FE-SEM (inset) images of: (a) Pt(Imp) TiNT and (b) Pt(IE) TiNT. Fig. 2. UV-vis DRS spectra of: (a) P-25, (b) TiNT, (c) Pt(Imp)TiNT, and (d) Pt (IE) TiNT. Fig. 3. XRD patterns of: (a) TiNT as is, (b) Pt(Imp)TiNT, and (c) Pt(IE) TiNT used in the splitting of water for 36 h. Physico-chemical properties of TiNT and PtTiNTs Fig.6.O 2 evolution from the splitting of water over various photocatalysts under visible light with one sun. Fig.5.H 2 evolution from the splitting of water over various photocatalysts under visible light with one sun. We are greatfully acknowledge the support from Brain Korea 21 (BK21) program and Center for Ultramicrochemical Process System (CUPS).We thank to Mr. Jong-Gyun Kang for HR-TEM analysis in Jeon-ju branch of the Korea Basic Science Institute (KBSI).

light source.source: We report a new material, platinum ion-exchanged titania nanotubes (Pt(IE)TiNT) synthesized by hydrothermal method, was found to be active under visible light irradiation.The platinum nanoparticles well embeded in nanotube matrixes in the ionized samples were highly photoactive materials for the stoichiometric production of hydrogen and oxygen by the splitting of water under visible light at one sun, resulting in hydrogen evolution rates of 14.6 and 2.3 lmolh in aqueous methanol and pure water, respectively.
light source.context: We report a new material, platinum ion-exchanged titania nanotubes (Pt(IE)TiNT) synthesized by hydrothermal method, was found to be active under visible light irradiation.The platinum nanoparticles well embeded in nanotube matrixes in the ionized samples were highly photoactive materials for the stoichiometric production of hydrogen and oxygen by the splitting of water under visible light at one sun, resulting in hydrogen evolution rates of 14.6 and 2.3 lmolh in aqueous methanol and pure water, respectively.

light source.source: The photocatalytic water splitting reaction was carried out with 0.5 g of fine powder photocatalysts in an outer irradiation Pyrex cell (300 ml) reaction system under the visible light (k 400 nm) irradiation using an ozone free Xe arc lamp (300 W, Hamamatsu: L 2479) attached with UV cut filter (FSQ-GG400 minimum spectral range of transmittance k 1 (4 0 0) bought from Newport Corporation) at one sun (AM 1.5).
light source.context: The titania nanotube (TiNT) was prepared using a similar method to that described by Kasuga et al. 15, except the hydrothermal temperature in an aqueous NaOH (8 M, 100 ml) was maintained at 130 C for 20 h 14.The slurry was filtrated, washed, dried (80 C) and calcined (250 C) in air.Pt was introduced onto the TiNT using an ion exchange method, employing an aqueous ammonia solution of platinum tetraamine Pt(II) chloride hydrate (Pt(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 A H 2 O), at pH 10, with continuous stirring for 20 h.The Pt ion-exchanged nanotube powder was carefully washed with DI water and calcined at 250 C, which was denoted as Pt(IE)TiNT.For comparison, Pt metal was also introduced onto the nanotube using the incipient wetness method with an aqueous solution of hydrogen hexachloroplatinate(IV) acid (H 2 PtCl 6 A 6 H 2 O), with the same loading amount and then calcined at 250 C, which was denoted as Pt(Imp)TiNT. The photocatalytic water splitting reaction was carried out with 0.5 g of fine powder photocatalysts in an outer irradiation Pyrex cell (300 ml) reaction system under the visible light (k 400 nm) irradiation using an ozone free Xe arc lamp (300 W, Hamamatsu: L 2479) attached with UV cut filter (FSQ-GG400 minimum spectral range of transmittance k 1 (4 0 0) bought from Newport Corporation) at one sun (AM 1.5).The details of the photocatalytic water splitting procedures have been described in our previous work except the substitution of UV light source to visible light and the type of reactor used 12. The prepared samples were characterized by high resolution transmission electron micorscopy (HR-TEM), Philips TECNAI-F20, at accelerated voltage 160 kV), X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD, Rigaku X-ray diffractometer, operating at 40 kV and 40 mA using Cu Ka radiations), UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-vis DRS, Shimadzu-UV525).Photocurrent density was measured by using a scanning potentiostate (EG G273), with the software (EG GM542).BET surface area (Micromeritics ASAP 2100) was obtained with N 2 adsorption at temperature of 77.40 K.

light source.source: Fig.6.O 2 evolution from the splitting of water over various photocatalysts under visible light with one sun.
light source.context: In conclusion, the Pt ion-exchanged titania nanotube, Pt(IE)TiNT, was highly active for the stoichiometric production of hydrogen and oxygen, 14.6 (in methanol solution) and 2.3 (in pure water) lmol H 2 h from the splitting of water under visible light.Highly dispersed Pt clusters (about 2 nm size) were formed in the texture of the TiNT in Pt(IE)TiNT, which significantly reduced the band gap energy from 3.1 eV (bare TiNT) to 2.48 eV (Pt(IE)TiNT).However, the large aggregated Pt particles introduced into the Pt(Imp)TiNT, using the impregnated method, did not contribute to the reduced band gap energy, and resulted in no photocatalytic activity for the splitting of water under visible light. Fig. 1.HR-TEM and FE-SEM (inset) images of: (a) Pt(Imp) TiNT and (b) Pt(IE) TiNT. Fig. 2. UV-vis DRS spectra of: (a) P-25, (b) TiNT, (c) Pt(Imp)TiNT, and (d) Pt (IE) TiNT. Fig. 3. XRD patterns of: (a) TiNT as is, (b) Pt(Imp)TiNT, and (c) Pt(IE) TiNT used in the splitting of water for 36 h. Physico-chemical properties of TiNT and PtTiNTs Fig.6.O 2 evolution from the splitting of water over various photocatalysts under visible light with one sun. Fig.5.H 2 evolution from the splitting of water over various photocatalysts under visible light with one sun. We are greatfully acknowledge the support from Brain Korea 21 (BK21) program and Center for Ultramicrochemical Process System (CUPS).We thank to Mr. Jong-Gyun Kang for HR-TEM analysis in Jeon-ju branch of the Korea Basic Science Institute (KBSI).

From the X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku with Cu Ka radiation) patterns of TiNT and ionized Pt(IE)TiNT as shown in Fig. 3, no apparent structural differences were observed either before or after the photocatalytic reaction run (for 36 h) over platinum ionized Pt(IE)TiNT, indicating its high stability in the photocatalytic water splitting reaction.Strong and sharp anatase titania peaks were observed at the 2-theta's of 25.3, 38.0, 48.3 and 54.8, 55.8, 62.58, 67.21, 68.79, 75.18 corresponding to (1 0 1), (0 0 4), (2 0 0), (1 0 5), (2 1 1), (2 0 4), (1 1 6), (2 2 0), (2 1 5) phases, respectively and a rutile peak at the 2-theta of 28.8.As shown in Table 1, the BET (Micromeritics ASAP 2100) surface areas decreased slightly and significantly on the Pt(IE)TiNT and Pt(Imp)TiNT, respectively.Fig. 4 shows the photocurrent densities of the catalysts, as measured using a scanning potentiostate (EG G273), with the software (EG GM542), as reported elsewhere 17.The photocurrent densities were measured using the catalysts film as the working electrodes, the Pt wire as the counter electrode and an AgAgCl3 M NaCl reference electrode, in an electrochemical cell (10 ml), with pH 9, 0.1 M Na 2 SO 3 as the electrolyte, under visible light.As describe in the literature 17, photocurrent densities are proportional to the number of photo formed electrons.Bare TiNT shows very low photocurrent densities under one sun (100 mWcm 2 ).The photocurrent densities of Pt(IE)TiNT are much higher than those of Pt(Imp)TiNT, even though they contain similar amounts of platinum.The possibility of sulphate ion acts as scavenger and might hamper in the photocurrent generation can be deducted since the applied potential for the all the samples are the same (0.5 V), however in dark potential all samples response to almost zero.These results suggest that presence of platinum particles and their sizes in Pt(IE)TiNT matrix has much influence in photocurrent generation attributed to the nanosize of embedded platinum particles.As expected from the UV-DRS data, highly dispersed Pt clusters on Pt(IE)TiNT promoted a photoresponse under visible light, resulting in high photocurrent densities.However, a significant amount of photo excited electrons may be recombined on the large aggregated Pt clusters in Pt(imp)TiNT, which may act as the recombination centers 18.These photocurrent results were consistent with the photocatalytic activities required to produce hydrogen by the splitting of water under visible light, as shown in Fig. 5. Bare TiO 2 (P-25) and TiNT showed no activities for H 2 evolution in either pure water or aqueous methanol solution under visible light.Furthermore, just trace amounts of hydrogen were evolved with the Pt(Imp) TiNT and Pt(Imp)TiO 2 (P-25) in either aqueous methanol or pure water.However, it is noteworthy that the Pt ionized titania nanotube, Pt(IE)TiNT, was significantly active for the production of hydrogen under visible light, at rates of 14.6 and 2.3 lmol H 2 h in the aqueous methanol solution and pure water, respectively.The catalyst was not deactivated with over 36 h of running.The production of hydrogen stopped in the dark as the light was turned off, but resumed when the visible light was turned on again, indicating the hydrogen production is a photocatalytic process. The oxygen evolution rate with Pt(IE)TiNT was almost stoichiometric with the mole ratio of H 2 O 2 2. Bare TiO 2 (P-25) and TiNT showed no activities for O 2 evolution in either pure water or aqueous methanol solution under visible light (Fig. 6).Furthermore, just trace amount of O 2 was evolved with the Pt(Imp)TiNT and Pt(Imp)TiO 2 (P-25) in either aqueous methanol or pure water.However, it is noteworthy that the Pt ionized titania nanotube, Pt(IE)TiNT, release significant amount of O 2 under visible light, at rates of 7.3 and 1.1 lmol O 2 h in the aqueous methanol solution and pure water, respectively. Well dispersed Pt clusters into the TiNT texture, about 2 nm in size, reduced the band gap energy from 3.1 eV to 2.48 eV, which leads to a significant photoresponse under ca.500 nm visible light, resulting in a large number of photo excitons, as observed by the high photocurrent densities on the Pt(IE)TiNT.These exciton may be efficiently separated owing not only to the well dispersed Pt particles, which promote quick electron transfer, but also the reduced grain boundaries of the TiNT compared to nano particles.Oxygen deficiency (Table 1) in the TiNT is expected to generates a sub-band level over the valence band.The band gap transitions in Pt(IE)TiNT may be attributable to internal transitions in the Pt d shell.Influence of metal composite has shown to facilitate charge rectification in semiconductor nanostructures.The noble metal Pt can act as sink for photoinduced charge carriers, promotes interfacial charge transfer process and these metal nanoparticles are considered to be highly electronegative as their Fermi level can be shifted to negative potentials owing to charge accumulating effects.The introduced small Pt particles shift the Fermi level toward the conduction band, which leads to the reduced band gap energy.The Fermi level shift of a semiconductor particle to the conduction band is possible due to the accumulation of electrons.Therefore, this improves the overall energetics of the semiconductor system.A smaller sized Pt metal particle induces a greater shift in the E F than larger particles 19.However, large aggregated Pt particles in Pt(Imp) TiNT were located on the exterior surface of the TiNT, as shown in Fig. 1, resulting in no variation of the band structure and no photocatalytic activity for the splitting of water.The recombination of excitons may be drastically increased on the large aggregated Pt particles in Pt(Imp) TiNT, due to the significant Pt-Pt interaction 20 as well as the non-uniform distributions 20 of the Pt clusters, resulting in extremely low photo catalytic activities and photocurrent densities.Sodium ions may also act as recombination sites in Pt(Imp)TiNT, as reported by Fernandez et al. 21.

reaction medium.source: In conclusion, the Pt ion-exchanged titania nanotube, Pt(IE)TiNT, was highly active for the stoichiometric production of hydrogen and oxygen, 14.6 (in methanol solution) and 2.3 (in pure water) lmol H 2 h from the splitting of water under visible light.
reaction medium.context: In conclusion, the Pt ion-exchanged titania nanotube, Pt(IE)TiNT, was highly active for the stoichiometric production of hydrogen and oxygen, 14.6 (in methanol solution) and 2.3 (in pure water) lmol H 2 h from the splitting of water under visible light.Highly dispersed Pt clusters (about 2 nm size) were formed in the texture of the TiNT in Pt(IE)TiNT, which significantly reduced the band gap energy from 3.1 eV (bare TiNT) to 2.48 eV (Pt(IE)TiNT).However, the large aggregated Pt particles introduced into the Pt(Imp)TiNT, using the impregnated method, did not contribute to the reduced band gap energy, and resulted in no photocatalytic activity for the splitting of water under visible light. Fig. 1.HR-TEM and FE-SEM (inset) images of: (a) Pt(Imp) TiNT and (b) Pt(IE) TiNT. Fig. 2. UV-vis DRS spectra of: (a) P-25, (b) TiNT, (c) Pt(Imp)TiNT, and (d) Pt (IE) TiNT. Fig. 3. XRD patterns of: (a) TiNT as is, (b) Pt(Imp)TiNT, and (c) Pt(IE) TiNT used in the splitting of water for 36 h. Physico-chemical properties of TiNT and PtTiNTs Fig.6.O 2 evolution from the splitting of water over various photocatalysts under visible light with one sun. Fig.5.H 2 evolution from the splitting of water over various photocatalysts under visible light with one sun. We are greatfully acknowledge the support from Brain Korea 21 (BK21) program and Center for Ultramicrochemical Process System (CUPS).We thank to Mr. Jong-Gyun Kang for HR-TEM analysis in Jeon-ju branch of the Korea Basic Science Institute (KBSI).

reactor type.source: The photocatalytic water splitting reaction was carried out with 0.5 g of fine powder photocatalysts in an outer irradiation Pyrex cell (300 ml) reaction system
reactor type.context: The titania nanotube (TiNT) was prepared using a similar method to that described by Kasuga et al. 15, except the hydrothermal temperature in an aqueous NaOH (8 M, 100 ml) was maintained at 130 C for 20 h 14.The slurry was filtrated, washed, dried (80 C) and calcined (250 C) in air.Pt was introduced onto the TiNT using an ion exchange method, employing an aqueous ammonia solution of platinum tetraamine Pt(II) chloride hydrate (Pt(NH 3 ) 4 Cl 2 A H 2 O), at pH 10, with continuous stirring for 20 h.The Pt ion-exchanged nanotube powder was carefully washed with DI water and calcined at 250 C, which was denoted as Pt(IE)TiNT.For comparison, Pt metal was also introduced onto the nanotube using the incipient wetness method with an aqueous solution of hydrogen hexachloroplatinate(IV) acid (H 2 PtCl 6 A 6 H 2 O), with the same loading amount and then calcined at 250 C, which was denoted as Pt(Imp)TiNT. The photocatalytic water splitting reaction was carried out with 0.5 g of fine powder photocatalysts in an outer irradiation Pyrex cell (300 ml) reaction system under the visible light (k 400 nm) irradiation using an ozone free Xe arc lamp (300 W, Hamamatsu: L 2479) attached with UV cut filter (FSQ-GG400 minimum spectral range of transmittance k 1 (4 0 0) bought from Newport Corporation) at one sun (AM 1.5).The details of the photocatalytic water splitting procedures have been described in our previous work except the substitution of UV light source to visible light and the type of reactor used 12. The prepared samples were characterized by high resolution transmission electron micorscopy (HR-TEM), Philips TECNAI-F20, at accelerated voltage 160 kV), X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD, Rigaku X-ray diffractometer, operating at 40 kV and 40 mA using Cu Ka radiations), UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-vis DRS, Shimadzu-UV525).Photocurrent density was measured by using a scanning potentiostate (EG G273), with the software (EG GM542).BET surface area (Micromeritics ASAP 2100) was obtained with N 2 adsorption at temperature of 77.40 K.

The photocatalytic water splitting reaction was carried out with 0.5 g of fine powder photocatalysts in an outer irradiation Pyrex cell (300 ml) reaction system under the visible light (k 400 nm) irradiation using an ozone free Xe arc lamp (300 W, Hamamatsu: L 2479) attached with UV cut filter (FSQ-GG400 minimum spectral range of transmittance k 1 (4 0 0) bought from Newport Corporation) at one sun (AM 1.5).The details of the photocatalytic water splitting procedures have been described in our previous work except the substitution of UV light source to visible light and the type of reactor used 12. The prepared samples were characterized by high resolution transmission electron micorscopy (HR-TEM), Philips TECNAI-F20, at accelerated voltage 160 kV), X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD, Rigaku X-ray diffractometer, operating at 40 kV and 40 mA using Cu Ka radiations), UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-vis DRS, Shimadzu-UV525).Photocurrent density was measured by using a scanning potentiostate (EG G273), with the software (EG GM542).BET surface area (Micromeritics ASAP 2100) was obtained with N 2 adsorption at temperature of 77.40 K.
